2012: Merged with the Higher School of Arts plastiques et visuels de Mons to form ARTS².

Among its notable directors, Alex De Taeye (1931–1952) played a key role in elevating the institution to the rank of Royal Conservatory. 

Today, ARTS² offers training in three areas:

Music: instrumental, vocal, composition, conducting, etc.

Theatre and arts of speech.

Visual arts.

The programs range from bachelor's degree to master's degree, with pedagogical options and collaborations with renowned ensembles such as the Royal Chamber Orchestra of Wallonia and New Music. 

The Royal Conservatory of Mons, through its history and commitment to artistic training, continues to play a central role in the cultural life of the region.
en.Couvent des Soeurs Noires:
The Convent of the Black Sisters of Mons, located at 2 rue des Sœurs Noires, is an emblematic place rich in history, blending spirituality, architecture and culture. 

In the middle of the 15th century, the nuns of the Béguinage of Cantimpret received permission to pronounce the three religious vows, adopting thus the rule of Saint Augustine. Wearing a black scapulaire, they are nicknamed 'The Black Sisters'. In 1485, the Pope granted them a bull of approval. However, confronted with the opposition of the nuns of Sainte-Waudru, they were forced to leave the Béguinage. In 1498, they settled on the outskirts of the city, in a modest neighborhood, with the support of the magistrates of Mons and generous donors. 

From the moment of their arrival, the Black Sisters dedicated themselves to charitable works:

Assistance to the sick: They provide home care, especially during the pest epidemic, gaining admiration among the inhabitants.

Education: In the 18th century, they opened a school for young girls of modest condition.

Hospitality: They offer hospitality to 'ladies in chambers', elderly women or isolated.

The convent underwent several transformations over the centuries:

Main chapel: Dedicated in 1516, it presents a single nave with a three-sided chevet.

Chapel of Sainte-Marie-Madeleine: Built in 1586, it is crowned with a bulbous campanile dated 1715.

Conventual buildings: Arranged around a cloister, they include a classical building built in 1749, characterized by a facade in brick and stone on two levels.

The convent is classified as a monument since November 13, 1987.

In 1584, Jeanne Féry, a nun of the convent, was at the center of a case of demonic possession. After a successful exorcism, a second chapel was built by the Archbishop of Cambrai, Louis de Berlaymont, who was buried there.

The convent houses a rich mobile patrimony, often referred to as 'The Treasure of the Black Sisters', comprising:

Chasubles: A remarkable collection of liturgical vestments.

Artworks: Paintings, statues, pieces of goldsmithry and manuscripts.

Relics: Notably a reliquary containing the cranium of Dagobert II.

Today, the site is integrated into the Ateliers des FUCaM (UCLouvain Mons) and welcomes:

Concerts: The chapel, renowned for its exceptional acoustics, is a place of choice for recordings and concerts.

Exhibitions: Contemporary artworks are exhibited, perpetuating the memory of the Black Sisters.

The Convent of the Black Sisters is a place rich in history, blending religious, architectural and cultural patrimony, bearing witness to the commitment of the nuns in the life of Mons over the centuries.
en.Eglise Notre Dame de Messines:
The Church of Notre-Dame de Messines, located on the rue de Bertaimont in Mons, is an emblematic place blending history, spirituality and popular traditions. 

Originally, the church was known as Saint-Nicolas in Bertaimont, dedicated to Saint-Nicolas, patron of merchants. In the 12th century, a chapel dedicated to this saint was founded in what is now the rue du Trieu. In the 13th century, the parish was established outside, then inside the walls of the city. The current church, the former church of the Recollets, is located in the rue de Bertaimont. 

The history of the church took a turn at the beginning of the 17th century with the discovery of a painting representing the Virgin Mary in the cemetery surrounding the building. This painting quickly became an object of veneration, attracting numerous pilgrims after miracles were attributed to it. In 1622, the painting was moved to be placed on the altar of the church. This event marked the beginning of an annual pilgrimage in honor of Notre-Dame de Messines. 

The Ducasse of Messines is an annual folkloric festival organized in the Messines district of Mons. It mainly consists of a religious procession, a pageant with giants, a competition for the most beautiful bouquet of flowers for children and a market for flowers and folkloric objects specific to the Ducasse. This festival, which has evolved from the initial pilgrimage, is celebrated on Saturday and Sunday closest to March 25. It is recognized as a masterpiece of oral and intangible heritage of the Federation Wallonia-Brussels since 2010. 

The current church, rebuilt in 1674 for security reasons, presents a sober architecture with a facade in blue stone and brick, typical of the region. It still houses the miraculous painting of the Virgin Mary. 

The church is open to the faithful and visitors, especially during religious ceremonies and events related to the Ducasse. 

The Church of Notre-Dame de Messines is a place rich in history and spirituality, bearing witness to the wealth of the religious and cultural heritage of Mons.
en.Eglise Sainte Elisabeth:
The Church of Sainte-Élisabeth of Mons, located at 18 rue de Nimy, is an emblematic building blending the styles of Gothic hennuyer and Baroque. At a stone's throw from the Grand-Place, it bears witness to the evolution of architecture and religion in the city since the 14th century. 

In 1345, Isabeau d'Antoing, widow of Gérard de Werchin, donated her private hotel to establish a chapel dedicated to her patron saint, Élisabeth of Hungary. This chapel gained importance, and despite the opposition of the clergy of the parish Saint-Germain, it was elevated to the status of parish in 1516 with the approval of the chapter of Saint-Germain. The construction of the Gothic church began at that time, and it was consecrated on October 16, 1588. 

On March 25, 1714, during the celebration of the Annunciation, a fire broke out in the church. The restoration was entrusted to the Mons architect Claude-Joseph de Bettignies, who preserved the gothic elements spared by the fire, such as the columns and a lateral facade, and reconstructed the rest in Baroque style. The new campanile was completed in 1720. 

Dimensions: 62 meters long and 29 meters wide. 

Nave: Three separated by sixteen Gothic pillars. 

Side altars: Five altars with neo-gothic polychrome retables. 

Facade: Ornated with figures in stone representing a knight and a siren. 

Verrières: 44 stained glass windows, including four historical ones. 

Campanile: Built in 1720, offering a panoramic view of the city. 

Châsse of Saint-George: Carrying the relics of the saint, it is accompanied since 2015 by a contemporary icon. 

Organ: Built in 1778, rebuilt in 1849 by Joseph Merklin. Although silent for several years, it remains a precious witness to the craftsmanship of Belgian organ music from the 19th century.

The campanile, designed by Claude-Joseph de Bettignies, houses a remarkable carillon. Guided tours allow visitors to discover this emblematic bell tower and admire an unobstructed view of Mons. 


The Church of Sainte-Élisabeth is a place rich in history, blending architectural wealth and religious patrimony. Its visit offers a plunge into the history of Mons and its evolution over the centuries. 

📍 Practical information
Address: 18 rue de Nimy, 7000 Mons, Belgium.

Access: Near the Grand-Place.

Opening hours: Open every day from 9am to 6pm (10am on Sunday). Closed during offices.

Contact: Office of Tourism of Mons at +32 65 33 55 80 or info.tourisme@ville.Mons.be.
en.Eglise Saint Nicolas:
The Church of Saint-Nicolas-en-Havré is one of the Baroque jewels of Mons, rich in a history of over 800 years and an exceptional artistic heritage. 

The parish of Saint-Nicolas was established in 1224, becoming the first parish located outside the walls of the city of Mons. It was initially linked to a hospital dedicated to Saint-Nicolas, patron of merchants. A first chapel was built, replaced in 1449 by a Gothic church. Unfortunately, this latter was destroyed by a fire in 1664, leaving only the bell-turret. The current church was rebuilt between 1664 and 1702 under the direction of the Mons architect Vincent Anthony, in Baroque style.

Baroque facade: The brick facade is adorned with calcareous stone and presents two distinct parts, each topped with curved pediments typical of the Baroque.

Bell-turret: Located at the left front of the building, it is built in grès de Bray and rises on five levels, topped with an octagonal spire. 

Interior: The church houses a sanctuary and a Baroque style altar, as well as 23 lateral chapels bearing witness to the wealth of the confraternities and corporations of the time. 

Organ: The romantic organ, whose buffet is classified, has been restored and benefits from an outstanding acoustics. 

Contemporary verrière: In 2013, a monumental verrière representing a stylized blue bird, the work of the master glassmaker Bernard Tirtiaux, was installed above the entrance portal. 

The church is a lively place, regularly hosting concerts, exhibitions and cultural events. It notably celebrated in 2024 the 800th anniversary of the parish with conferences, organ concerts and guided tours. 

The Church of Saint-Nicolas-en-Havré is a living testimony to the history of Mons, blending architectural wealth, artistic heritage and cultural dynamism.
en.Gare de Mons:
The Mons railway station is a striking example of architectural transformation and public controversy. Since it was first brought into service in 1841, the station has undergone several reconstructions, the most recent of which was designed by the Spanish architect Santiago Calatrava.

1841: Opening of the first Mons railway station.

1874: Construction of a new building in Neo-Romanesque style, featuring a large iron and glass canopy.

1952: Inauguration of a modern station designed by architect René Panis, decorated with a mural by Jacques D'Hondt depicting the socio-cultural life of the region.

2013: Demolition of the 1952 station to make way for an ambitious project signed by Santiago Calatrava.


Initially scheduled for completion in 2015, Calatrava’s project aimed to create an iconic bridge-station linking the historic centre of Mons to the Grands Prés district. However, the construction site accumulated nine years of delay, and the budget rose from 37 million to almost 480 million euros, sparking strong criticism regarding the management of the project.

The station was nicknamed “the dragon” because of its originally planned shape, but some now compare it to a dragonfly.

Mons station is an important railway hub, served by InterCity (IC) trains, Omnibus (L) trains, Peak Hour (P) services, and the international Ouigo Train Classique (OTC), which connects Paris to Brussels with a stop in Mons.

It also offers connections with TEC buses and has parking facilities for bicycles and cars, making intermodal travel easier.

Mons railway station illustrates the challenges of modernising public infrastructure, where architectural ambition and budget management must be kept in balance. Despite the controversies, it remains a central point of the Belgian railway network and a symbol of the city of Mons.

In 2025, the new Mons station was ranked among the seven most beautiful railway stations in the world by the Prix Versailles, a prestigious international architecture award. Imagined by Spanish architect Santiago Calatrava, it is distinguished by its large glass-and-steel footbridge linking the historic centre to the new districts. Its bright white structure gives the impression of a true contemporary cathedral dedicated to rail. This recognition places Mons on the world map of cities with remarkable architectural heritage and makes the station a truly iconic gateway to the region.
en.Gillis:
Marcel Gillis (Mons 1897 - Mons 1972)

Biography

Painter, illustrator, restorer, caricaturist, poet and popular singer, Marcel Gillis has a predilection for still lifes, landscapes, portraits and religious compositions. He also draws inspiration from everyday life in the Borinage and popular culture he represents with spirit, often with humor, sometimes with irony, but always with a touch of sentimentality. Figurative, his work falls within the realist current of Wallonia and approaches the artistic circle Nervia. 

Despite the outbreak of the First World War, the young Gillis pursued a brilliant education at the Academy of Fine Arts of Mons from 1913 to 1923. He began exhibiting in 1922 at the Tournai salon and the triennial exhibition in Antwerp a year later. Named curator of the Museum of Fine Arts of Mons (1928-1965), cofounder of the artistic circle Les Loups (with A. Delaunois, P. Dequene, A. Detry, A. Jacqmotte and P. Joris) and member of the Cercle Bon Vouloir, Marcel Gillis is a prominent figure in the cultural life of Mons for nearly half a century, actively contributing to the development of artistic, literary and folkloric activities. 

Complete artist, Gillis is a local figure, a talented incarnation of the Montois soul who expresses himself best in the festivities of the Ducasse of the Trinité. We owe him the famous Tableau d'Procession: a Montois patois song recounting the Sunday of the Trinité at Mons, and this on multiple airs very familiar to French and popular songs.
en.Le Car d Or:
The Car d'Or is a central element of the Montois heritage, at the heart of the Ducasse of Mons, also known as 'the Doudou', a folkloric festival inscribed since 2005 in the intangible heritage of humanity by UNESCO. 

The tradition of the Car d'Or dates back to the 14th century. In 1349, when the plague ravaged the region, the authorities of Mons organized a solemn procession to implore the protection of Sainte Waudru. The relics of the saint were transported to the Bruyères of Casteau, where they met those of Saint Vincent, her husband. After this homage, the epidemic decreased, and the Montois decided to organize an annual procession in honor of Sainte Waudru. 

The current Car d'Or, a procession carriage in wood carved, painted and gilded, was made between 1779 and 1782 in the style of Louis XVI. It is the only procession carriage of this period still in use today. 

Each year, on the Sunday of the Trinité, the case containing the relics of Sainte Waudru is placed on the Car d'Or. Pulled by six horses of draft and accompanied by more than 1,500 participants in period costumes, the carriage travels through the streets of Mons during a solemn procession. Readings of miracles attributed to the saint are made at different emblematic places in the city. 

The climax of the procession is the ascent of the Car d'Or on the paved ramp of the Collegiate Sainte-Waudru. According to tradition, if the carriage does not reach the summit in one go, a misfortune will strike the city that year. This ascent, although symbolic, mobilizes an enthusiastic crowd pushing the carriage to ensure its ascent without interruption. 

The fake Car d'Or of 2009: Due to European elections, the Ducasse was postponed. However, a private individual, Jean-Michel Maurage, built a replica of the Car d'Or and organized a symbolic ascent of the ramp, perpetuating the tradition despite the circumstances. 

The chronograms: The Car d'Or is adorned with cartouches bearing inscriptions in Latin, called chronograms, commemorating historical events related to the saint and the city. 

Period of release: It was released once a year, during the procession of the Sunday of the Trinité, within the framework of the Ducasse of Mons. 

The Car d'Or is much more than just a procession carriage; it embodies identity, faith and resilience of the Montois across the centuries. Its annual release is a moment of unity and popular fervor, bringing together residents and visitors around a living and vibrant tradition. 

The Car d'Or is usually exhibited in the Collegiate Sainte-Waudru, in Mons.
en.Lucie et les Papillons:
« Lucie et les Papillons » is a monumental sculpture installed in 2019 on the Franklin Delano Roosevelt Square in Mons. Created by the French artist David Mesguich, it represents a kneeling girl holding a broken pair of scissors, surrounded by butterflies. 

The sculpture is inspired by the artist's daughter, Lucie, and the expression 'having butterflies in the belly', symbolizing the budding love. The broken pair of scissors in the girl's hands evokes the fragility of feelings and the innocence of childhood. This poetic representation invites reflection on the purity of emotions and the fragility of human nature. 

Measuring more than 7 meters high and composed of nearly 2,000 facets in stainless steel, the sculpture is the result of a collaboration between the artist, the Social Promotion School of Jemappes-Quiévrain, Façozinc and WAX Architecture. It illustrates the fusion between digital techniques and traditional modeling, characteristics of Mesguich's work. 

« Lucie et les Papillons » is part of the project « L'Art habite la Ville », aiming to integrate art into the public space of Mons. It questions the place of humanity in urban environment, physical and symbolic boundaries, and invites us to rethink our relationship with the city. 

This sculpture, combining contemporary aesthetics and universal message, has become a strong symbol of Mons, attracting both residents and visitors. It embodies the encounter between art, emotion and public space.
en.Machine a Eau:
The Water Machine of Mons is an emblematic building of the 19th century industrial engineering, located on the Boulevard Dolez, near the city center and now integrated into the Mons Memorial Museum (MMM). 

Following the severe cholera epidemics of the 19th century, the city of Mons, like many others in Europe, became aware of the importance of a water supply network. Until then, the inhabitants had been drawing water from contaminated wells or the Trouille river, unwholesome. 

1865: the municipal council approved the creation of a hydraulic plant. 

1869-1871: construction of the Water Machine according to the plans of the engineer Jules Drion and the architect Joseph Hubert. 

The system exploited the force of the Trouille river to pump water from the sources of Spiennes to the distribution reservoirs of the city. 

The water naturally descended from Spiennes to the Machine. 

A hydraulic wheel (then a steam engine as a supplement) powered the pumps. 

The water was then pushed into a reservoir located under the belfry garden, which redistributed the water in Mons. 

This system not only provided domestic water supply but also public fountains and fire fighting. 

In 1961, the Trouille river was definitively diverted. 

In 1974, the Water Machine was decommissioned. 

In 1977, the building was classified as a monument by the Walloon Region for its patrimonial interest. 

The building was renovated in the 1990s and completely rehabilitated to become in 2015 the Mons Memorial Museum (MMM), at the occasion of Mons European Capital of Culture. 

The MMM is a museum dedicated to the military history of the Middle Ages to the present day, with a focus on the Second World War and the First World War, especially the Battle of Mons of 1914. 

It also contains a memorial to the Shoah, a collection of military objects, uniforms, archives, and audiovisual testimonies. 

The Water Machine has long been overlooked by the general public, despite its capital importance in the history of health care of the city. 

The building served temporarily for various uses before being restored: warehouse, garage, among others. 

Its hydraulic wheel of 4.80 meters in diameter, now disappeared, was one of the largest in the region at the time. 

The appearance of the building (red brick, glazed arcades, high chimneys) makes a fine example of inspired industrial architecture from the style of neo-classical and industrial 19th century. 

The Water Machine of Mons is not only a technical feat of the past, it is also a symbol of modernity for its time and today a place of memory reinvented in the heart of the Montois landscape.
en.Maison Espagnole:
The Spanish House of Mons, located at 23 rue des Clercs, is an architectural jewel of the city, nestled at the foot of the Belfry. Built between the second half of the 16th century and the beginning of the 17th century, it bears witness to the reconstruction of Mons after the great fire of 1548, which ravaged a large part of the city. 

The Spanish House is a typical example of the architecture of the old Netherlands, often called 'scaldienne'. It stands out by its red brick facade, its steps (or no steps of the monk) and its windows at cross. The tiled roof with steep pitch is pierced with lucarnes also at steps. The rear facade, attached to the Ramp of the Chateau, includes only two levels. The entire building was classified as a monument on November 10, 1955. 

Over the centuries, the Spanish House has undergone several assignments. In the 20th century, it housed the Press House for about 30 years. Restoration work has been undertaken to stabilize the building and renovate the exterior joinery. 

Since October 2022, the Spanish House has been hosting the UNESCO World Heritage House, a dedicated interpretation center for sites and traditions of Mons and the region recognized by UNESCO. This unique Belgian site offers visitors an immersion into the rich cultural heritage of the region, including:

The Ducasse of Mons (Doudou)

The Belfry of Mons

The Grand-Hornu site

The Mundaneum

The Neolithic silex mines of Spiennes

The skill of the carillonners

The UNESCO World Heritage House proposes interactive exhibitions, workshops, conferences and meetings, aiming to raise public awareness of the preservation of cultural heritage.
en.Maison Losseau:
The Maison Losseau, located at 37 rue de Nimy in Mons, is a masterpiece of Art Nouveau Belgian and an exceptional witness to the modernity of the beginning of the 20th century. 

Léon Losseau (1869–1949), lawyer, bibliophile, numismatist and patron of the city, inherited the family home acquired by his father in 1873. From 1899, he began a radical transformation of the residence, entrusting the work to the architect Paul Saintenoy, a major figure in Art Nouveau in Belgium. The finishing touches were made by the Parisian architects Henri Sauvage and Charles Sarazin, then continued by the Brusselsian Louis Sauvage. The work extended until 1913, at which point the house became the first in Mons equipped with electricity and central heating with charcoal. 

Behind a discreet neoclassical facade hides a sumptuous interior, where each room is decorated according to a floral theme. For example, the entrance hall is adorned with roses and orchids. The salon is crowned with a carved wooden verrière, decorated in 1911 with 15 panes of glass of Amalric Walter representing the beffroi of Thuin and the Sambre river. Wikipédia

Losseau also integrated advanced technological innovations for the time: an elevator, hand cranks, a telephone line installed in 1905, as well as a central hydraulical heating system with grilles for better diffusion of heat. 

Today, the Maison Losseau houses an interpretation center dedicated to the collections of Léon Losseau and to the literary innuyère. Among the treasures preserved are more than 100,000 volumes, mainly devoted to politics, literature and poetry, as well as a collection of more than 10,000 medals. 

An outstanding feature of Losseau's life is the rediscovery in 1901 of the original edition of Une Saison en enfer d'Arthur Rimbaud, which we believed had been destroyed. An exhibition room is dedicated to this major literary event. 

During the First World War, the house was requisitioned by Prince Rupprecht of Bavaria and transformed into a general headquarters. Losseau was forced to leave his residence, which suffered damage. He received compensation in 1933 for the damages suffered. During the Second World War, the house was again occupied, its library was pillaged and its collections vandalized. Losseau undertook renovations until his death in 1949. 

Classified as exceptional heritage of Wallonia (facade and roofs in 1982, interior in 1983), the Maison Losseau is the subject of an important restoration between 2011 and 2015, in anticipation of Mons European Capital of Culture. She reopened her doors to the public in 2015, offering an immersion into the refined universe of Léon Losseau. 

The Maison Losseau is an essential stop for fans of Art Nouveau, history and literature, offering a unique immersion into the elegance and innovation of the beginning of the 20th century.
en.Maison Rue de Bertaimont 17:
The house located at 17 rue de Bertaimont in Mons is a classified building of Wallonia heritage since August 12, 1988 . Elle se distingue par sa façade en pierre bleue, ses grandes fenêtres à arcs surbaissés et sa toiture à deux versants, caractéristiques de l'architecture montoise du XVIIIe siècle. Bien que les détails spécifiques de son histoire soient limités, sa situation dans le quartier historique de Messines suggère une intégration dans le tissu urbain ancien de la ville.

The Messines district, where the rue de Bertaimont is located, is known for its annual Ducasse, a folkloric festival dating from 1620, depicting giants and traditional objects. The house at number 17 may have been a witness to these festivities over the centuries.

Today, the building is used for residential and commercial purposes, illustrating the contemporary reuse of historic buildings in the city center.
en.Maison Rue de Bertaimont 33:
The house located at 33 rue de Bertaimont in Mons is a remarkable building of Montois architectural heritage, classified as a monument since August 12, 1988. 

Built in the 18th century, this house in stone consists of three levels and three bays. The facade, of Montois style, presents a peculiarity: the right bay is slightly recessed, wider, and pierced by a door and a balcony. The openings are crowned with lintels intradossés and simple at the floors, with very long keys. The voussoirs are adorned with lozenges or cut panels. The house is crowned with a front-pitched roof pierced with a lucarne. 

Located in the Messines district, this house may have been a witness to numerous historical events in Mons. For example, during the siege of Mons in 1691 by the troops of Louis XIV, the gate of Bertaimont, located nearby, was a strategic point attacked by French forces. 

The Messines district is also known for its annual Ducasse, a folkloric festival dating from 1620, depicting giants and traditional objects. The house at number 33 may have been a witness to these festivities over the centuries. 

The house at 33 rue de Bertaimont is a typical example of the residential Montois architecture of the 18th century, bearing witness to the rich and varied history of the city of Mons.
en.Maison Rue de la Couronne 20 22:
The house located at numbers 20-22 of the rue de la Couronne in Mons is a remarkable building of the historic center of Montois, bearing witness to the residential architecture of the 18th century. Classified as a monument since July 28, 1989, elle est inscrite au patrimoine immobilier de Wallonie. 

This house stands out by its facade of four bays rising on three levels. The ground floor, partially transformed, presents a masonry in brick and stone typical of the style of the time, while the upper floors are plastered. The windows have preserved part of their original small wood elements, characteristic of the old joinery. The roof is a tiled front-pitched roof, covered with a lucarne, adding to the charm of the ensemble. 

The rue de la Couronne is one of the oldest streets in Mons, connecting the Grand-Place to the rue de Nimy. It is lined with several classified buildings, including numbers 4-6, 10-12 and 16, forming a homogeneous architectural ensemble and representative of the urban evolution of the city. The presence of these buildings bears witness to the historical and patrimonial wealth of this street. 

The house at numbers 20-22 is protected for its facades, roof and lucarnes, ensuring the preservation of its remarkable architectural elements. This protection aims to maintain the integrity of the building and to enhance the built heritage of Mons. 

The house is not open to the public, but its facade can be admired from the street. It constitutes an interesting stop for amateurs of architecture and history during a walk in the historic center of Mons. 

The House of the rue de la Couronne 20-22 is a significant example of the architectural heritage of Montois, illustrating the history of the Montois residential life in the 18th century. Its conservation contributes to the richness of cultural and historical heritage of Mons.
en.Maison Rue de Nimy 53:
The house located at 53 rue de Nimy in Mons is a remarkable building, classified as a monument since May 30, 1989. It stands out for its typical Tournai-style architecture, characterized by upper-floor bays with bands at the level of projecting sills and above the arches. A brick entablature under a band and a molded stone cornice support a hipped roof, lit by two dormer windows with curved pediments.

Currently, this building houses the headquarters of IDEA, the Territorial Development Agency of the Heart of Hainaut, which plays a key role in the economic and territorial development of the region.

Located near major cultural sites such as the Mundaneum and the Maison Losseau, the house at 53 rue de Nimy is part of an environment rich in heritage and history.

For lovers of architecture and history, this house represents a significant example of Mons heritage, combining architectural elegance and institutional importance.
en.Marche aux Herbes:
The Place du Marché aux Herbes in Mons, often called the 'Petit Marché', is an iconic spot in the city center, rich in history and local life.

Before 1409, Mons had only one market area, the Grand-Marché, which extended over several streets in the city center. In 1409, to better organize commercial activities, a specific market for foodstuffs, called the Marché de Coppenage or Marché des Mois à Fromages, was established. This market was then moved to its current location in the 17th century, taking the name Marché aux Herbes.

A fountain, known as the Fontaine du Rouge-Puits, was installed on the square until 1955. Its name comes either from its red color or from the water tinted by the cleaning of meat by butchers. It was later moved to the entrance of the pedestrian area.

Today, the Place du Marché aux Herbes is a lively place, especially popular with young people and students. It is lined with many bars, restaurants, and terraces, making it a center of Mons' nightlife.

The square also hosts various cultural and festive events throughout the year, reinforcing its central role in the social life of Mons.

The square is surrounded by historic buildings, witnesses to the city's architectural evolution. Among them are facades dating from the 18th century, illustrating the classic Montois style.

The Place du Marché aux Herbes is thus a place where history, culture, and conviviality meet, reflecting the vibrant soul of Mons.
en.Mayeur:
The Jardin du Mayeur: A green escape in the heart of Mons

Behind the Town Hall, the Jardin du Mayeur offers a peaceful getaway in the middle of the city. This haven of greenery, rich in vegetation and history, invites you to discover its surprising sculpture and its century-old trees. A perfect place to relax, listen to the belfry carillon, or enjoy open-air concerts.

Nestled behind the Town Hall, the Jardin du Mayeur is an oasis of greenery, landscaped between 1930 and 1936. This small, wooded haven offers a moment of calm in the very heart of the city.

Here you will find the famous Ropieur sculpture (Gober, 1937), a fountain that surprises passersby, as well as a beautiful collection of century-old trees: copper beech, lime trees, horse chestnut, paulownia, and other lush vegetation.

Exiting via rue d'Enghien, let yourself be guided by the Victor Hugo passage, a bucolic path that leads you straight to rue Margerite Bervoets, just a few steps from the majestic Belfry site.

The Jardin du Mayeur is also a privileged place to listen to the belfry carillon, and its open-air concerts are a real treat for the ears.
en.Mundaneum:
The Mundaneum is an archive center and museum space located in Mons, often nicknamed the 'paper Google' due to its visionary ambition to gather and organize all human knowledge.

Founded at the end of the 19th century by two Belgian lawyers, Paul Otlet (1868–1944) and Henri La Fontaine (1854–1943), the Mundaneum aimed to collect and classify all the world's knowledge according to the Universal Decimal Classification (UDC), a system they developed and which is still used today in many libraries. Henri La Fontaine, a fervent pacifist, received the Nobel Peace Prize in 1913 for his commitment to world peace. Their initial project, known as the Universal Bibliographic Repertoire (UBR), was launched in 1895.

The Mundaneum preserves approximately 12 million bibliographic cards, representing a monumental attempt to catalog all world publications. Its collections also include:

The International Press Museum, with newspapers from around the world.

Archives on pacifism, feminism, and anarchism.

Iconographic documents such as posters, postcards, photographs, and glass plates.

The Mondothèque, a multimedia desk concept imagined by Paul Otlet, prefiguring personal computers and digital tablets.

Initially installed in Brussels in the Cinquantenaire Palace, the Mundaneum was moved to Mons in 1998, where it is now housed in an Art Deco building. The museum's scenography was entrusted to comic book authors François Schuiten and Benoît Peeters, offering a unique immersion into the founders' universe.

The Mundaneum received the European Heritage Label in 2016 and is inscribed in UNESCO's Memory of the World program, highlighting its importance in the history of documentation and information.

In 2012, the Mundaneum established a partnership with Google to promote its heritage as a precursor to modern search engines. The center regularly organizes temporary exhibitions on various themes, such as coding, cryptography, and social movements.
en.Musee du Doudou:
The Musée du Doudou in Mons is an essential place to understand the cultural identity of the city and its attachment to the Ducasse de Mons, a folk festival recognized as intangible heritage of humanity by UNESCO since 2005.

Located in the Jardin du Mayeur, behind the Town Hall, the museum is housed in the former Mont-de-Piété, a historic building restored to accommodate this cultural institution.

The museum offers a modern and interactive scenography that immerses visitors in the heart of the Ducasse de Mons. Here you can discover the legendary battle between Saint George and the Dragon, called the Lumeçon, which takes place every year on the Grand-Place. Multimedia devices, objects from the communal collections, and artistic installations help understand the origins, symbols, and evolution of this festival.

The Musée du Doudou addresses all audiences: families, school groups, individual visitors. Activity booklets are available for children, guided tours are offered in several languages, and educational workshops are organized throughout the year. The museum is also accessible to people with reduced mobility and offers adaptations for visually and hearing impaired people.

Adjacent to the museum, the Jardin du Mayeur is a peaceful green space in the heart of the city. Created in 1930, it houses the famous Fontaine du Ropieur, symbol of the rebellious spirit of the Montois.

The Musée du Doudou is much more than just a museum: it's a gateway to the Montois soul, a living celebration of a centuries-old tradition that continues to bring together residents and visitors around shared values of sharing, celebration, and memory.
en.Palais de Justice:
The Mons Courthouse, located in the heart of the city, is an imposing building that embodies the judicial power and architectural heritage of Mons. Built in the 19th century, it stands as a symbol of justice and the rule of law in the region.

The courthouse was constructed between 1866 and 1870, designed by architect Joseph Jonas Dumont in a neoclassical style. The building features a monumental facade with columns, pediments, and classical architectural elements that reflect the solemnity and importance of the judicial institution it houses.

The courthouse serves as the seat of the Court of First Instance, the Commercial Court, and the Labor Court for the judicial district of Mons. It plays a crucial role in the administration of justice in the Hainaut province, handling civil, criminal, and commercial cases.

The building's interior includes courtrooms, offices for judges and court staff, waiting areas, and administrative spaces. The main courtroom, with its high ceilings and formal atmosphere, is designed to inspire respect for the judicial process.

The Mons Courthouse is not only a functional judicial building but also an important part of the city's architectural heritage. Its presence in the urban landscape reminds citizens and visitors alike of the importance of justice and the legal system in maintaining social order.

The courthouse continues to serve its original purpose while adapting to modern judicial needs, ensuring that justice is accessible to all citizens of the Mons region.
en.Parc du Waux Hall:
The Parc du Waux-Hall is one of the most beautiful green spaces in Mons, offering visitors a haven of peace and nature in the heart of the city. This historic park, created in the 19th century, combines natural beauty with cultural heritage.

The park was designed in the English landscape style, characterized by winding paths, varied vegetation, and picturesque viewpoints. It covers several hectares and features a large pond, century-old trees, flower beds, and open spaces perfect for relaxation and outdoor activities.

The park is home to several notable features: a bandstand where concerts are held during the summer months, walking paths that wind through the greenery, and various species of trees and plants that create a diverse ecosystem. The large pond, fed by natural springs, is a focal point of the park and attracts various species of birds.

Throughout the year, the Parc du Waux-Hall hosts various cultural events, including outdoor concerts, exhibitions, and seasonal celebrations. During the summer, it becomes a popular gathering place for families, joggers, and nature lovers.

The park's name comes from the English term 'Vauxhall', referring to pleasure gardens, and reflects the 19th-century fashion for creating urban green spaces inspired by English landscape design.

The Parc du Waux-Hall is not only a recreational space but also an important part of Mons' green heritage, contributing to the city's quality of life and environmental balance. It offers visitors a perfect escape from urban life while remaining easily accessible from the city center.
en.Pavillons Caserne Cavalerie:
The Cavalry Barracks Pavilions in Mons are historic military buildings that bear witness to the city's strategic importance and military heritage. These buildings, constructed in the 19th century, served as accommodation and training facilities for cavalry units stationed in Mons.

The pavilions were built during the period when Mons was a major military garrison, particularly under Dutch rule and later under Belgian independence. The architecture reflects the military construction standards of the time, with functional design, solid materials, and practical layouts suited to military needs.

The buildings feature typical military architecture with symmetrical facades, large courtyards for training and parades, and stables for horses. The construction materials and techniques used were chosen for durability and functionality, reflecting the practical needs of military operations.

Over the years, the cavalry barracks played an important role in the defense of the region and served as a training center for military personnel. The presence of these facilities contributed to Mons' development as a military and administrative center.

Today, while the original military function has evolved, the pavilions remain as important architectural and historical landmarks. They represent a significant period in Mons' history when the city was a key military stronghold in the region.

The Cavalry Barracks Pavilions are part of Mons' rich military heritage and contribute to the understanding of the city's strategic importance throughout history. They stand as reminders of the military traditions that have shaped the development and character of Mons over the centuries.
en.Place du Parc:
The Place du Parc in Mons is a historic square that serves as an important junction in the city's urban fabric. Located near the Collegiate Church of Sainte-Waudru, this square has played a significant role in the city's development and continues to be a vital part of Mons' urban landscape.

The square is characterized by its strategic location and architectural ensemble, which includes both historic buildings and modern elements. It serves as a transition space between different parts of the city, connecting the historic center with other important areas of Mons.

The Place du Parc is surrounded by buildings of various architectural styles, reflecting the evolution of the city over time. Some buildings date from the medieval period, while others were constructed or renovated in more recent centuries. This mix of styles creates a unique urban character that tells the story of Mons' architectural development.

The square serves multiple functions: it's a transportation hub, a meeting point, and a space for various activities and events. Its central location makes it easily accessible and a natural gathering place for residents and visitors alike.

Throughout the year, the Place du Parc may host various events, markets, or cultural activities, contributing to the vibrant life of the city. Its open space provides a welcome break from the more densely built-up areas of the historic center.

The Place du Parc is not just a physical space but also a symbol of Mons' urban identity, representing the city's ability to adapt and evolve while preserving its historical character. It continues to serve as an important landmark and meeting point in the heart of Mons.
en.Porte Rue Courte:
The Porte Rue Courte is a historic gateway in Mons that represents an important element of the city's medieval fortifications. This gate, along with other defensive structures, once formed part of the protective system that surrounded the medieval city of Mons.

The gate was constructed during the medieval period when Mons was a fortified city, and it served as one of the main entry points to the urban center. Its strategic location and defensive design were essential for controlling access to the city and protecting its inhabitants from potential threats.

The architecture of the Porte Rue Courte reflects the military construction techniques of the medieval period. Built with solid materials such as stone and brick, the gate was designed to withstand attacks while providing controlled access to the city. The structure likely included defensive features such as arrow slits, battlements, and possibly a portcullis.

Over the centuries, as the need for defensive fortifications diminished, many of Mons' medieval gates were demolished or modified. The Porte Rue Courte, however, has been preserved as an important historical landmark, serving as a reminder of the city's medieval past and its strategic importance in the region.

Today, the gate stands as a testament to Mons' rich history and architectural heritage. It provides visitors with a tangible connection to the medieval period and helps them understand the city's development from a fortified settlement to a modern urban center.

The Porte Rue Courte is not just a historical monument but also a symbol of Mons' resilience and its ability to preserve its heritage while adapting to changing times. It continues to be an important landmark that contributes to the city's unique character and historical identity.
en.Prison de Mons:
The Mons Prison is a historic correctional facility that has played a significant role in the city's judicial and social history. Built in the 19th century, this institution has served as a place of detention and rehabilitation for over a century, reflecting the evolution of penal systems and social attitudes toward crime and punishment.

The prison was constructed during a period when European societies were developing more systematic approaches to criminal justice and incarceration. The building's design reflects the architectural and philosophical principles of the time, with features intended to facilitate surveillance, control, and the rehabilitation of inmates.

The facility has housed various categories of prisoners throughout its history, from those serving short sentences to long-term inmates. The prison's operations have evolved over time to reflect changing approaches to criminal justice, including the introduction of educational programs, vocational training, and other rehabilitation initiatives.

The Mons Prison is part of Belgium's broader correctional system and has contributed to the development of modern penal practices in the country. Its history provides insights into how societies have addressed issues of crime, punishment, and rehabilitation over time.

While the prison continues to serve its primary function, it also represents an important aspect of Mons' institutional heritage. The building stands as a reminder of the city's role in the administration of justice and the care of individuals within the criminal justice system.

The Mons Prison is not just a functional institution but also a historical landmark that reflects the evolution of social attitudes toward crime and punishment, as well as the development of modern correctional practices in Belgium.
en.Ropieur:
The Ropieur is one of the most emblematic characters of Mons folklore, a mischievous and beloved figure that embodies the playful spirit of the city. This legendary character is particularly associated with the Ducasse de Mons celebrations and has become a symbol of local identity and humor.

The Ropieur is typically represented as a small, mischievous figure who enjoys playing pranks and causing harmless trouble. In local folklore, he is often depicted as a small being who hides in the shadows and emerges during festivals and celebrations to spread joy and laughter among the crowds.

The character's name 'Ropieur' comes from the Walloon dialect and is related to the verb 'roper' which means to whisper or speak softly. This etymology reflects the character's nature as someone who operates discreetly, spreading rumors and gossip in a playful manner.

During the Ducasse de Mons, the Ropieur becomes particularly active, appearing in various forms throughout the celebrations. He is often represented in costumes, masks, and various artistic expressions, becoming a central figure in the festive atmosphere that characterizes the city's most important cultural event.

The Ropieur has inspired numerous artistic representations, including sculptures, paintings, and theatrical performances. His image can be found throughout the city, particularly in the Jardin du Mayeur where a famous sculpture by Gober (1937) represents this beloved character.

Beyond his role in folklore, the Ropieur represents the spirit of Mons itself - playful, welcoming, and full of life. He embodies the city's ability to maintain a sense of humor and joy even in difficult times, making him a cherished symbol of local culture and identity.
en.Tank Sherman:
The M4A1 Sherman tank "In the Mood" is an emblematic monument of Mons, located in front of the Mons Memorial Museum, on Boulevard Dolez. Installed in August 2019, it commemorates the 75th anniversary of the liberation of Mons by Allied forces in September 1944.

This Sherman tank, nicknamed "In the Mood" in reference to Glenn Miller's popular song, participated in the liberation battles of Belgium. It has been restored and installed as a commemorative monument to honor the memory of the Allied soldiers who liberated Mons and the region.

The tank is displayed outdoors, freely accessible to visitors, and constitutes a major point of interest at the Mons Memorial Museum site. It is part of the museum's visit route, which traces the military history of Mons from the Middle Ages to the present day.

The Sherman Tank "In the Mood" is a tangible testimony to Mons' military history and a symbol of gratitude towards the Allied forces who contributed to the liberation of Belgium during World War II.
en.Theatre Royal:
The Royal Theatre of Mons, located on the Grand-Place, is an iconic building of Mons' cultural heritage, combining history, architecture, and artistic life. Built between 1841 and 1843 based on the plans of Mons architect Charles Sury, the Royal Theatre adopts a neoclassical style characteristic of the era. It was inaugurated on October 18, 1843. At that time, Mons aimed to compete with other major Belgian cities in terms of cultural infrastructure. The project was designed by architect Joseph Hubert. The building was constructed in a neoclassical style, with an imposing façade adorned with columns. In 1869, a fire partially destroyed the building. Since then, it has undergone several renovation phases to meet modern technical standards, notably in 1948, 1997, 1998, 2006, and 2011. From the beginning, the Royal Theatre has hosted iconic theatre figures such as Sarah Bernhardt and has been the venue for many lyrical and dramatic performances. Today, it offers a varied program including concerts, musicals, comedy shows, and plays. The interior of the theatre is decorated with garnet velvet and gilding, typical of 'Italian-style' halls. The ceiling features panels representing allegories of tragedy, comedy, and vaudeville, as well as the four seasons. A notable work is the painted canvas by Alexandre Obolensky, depicting a trompe-l'œil sky, which guides visitors from the entrance hall to the lower bar. The theatre has an Italian-style auditorium, with wrought-iron balconies, gilded decor, and a ceiling adorned with frescoes.

It can accommodate around 500 spectators.

There is also a richly decorated foyer, often used for receptions, exhibitions, or chamber music concerts.

The cast iron doors of the theatre, created by ironworker Ph-J. Hoyois and decorated by sculptor Émile-Joseph Hoyaux, feature medallions representing Molière, Racine, Grétry, and Roland de Lassus, as well as the coat of arms of the city of Mons.

The theatre has hosted prestigious performances of classical theatre, opera, and ballet. It was both a social and cultural venue.

During World War II, the theatre was briefly closed and used by the German occupiers for propaganda performances.

It has been renovated multiple times, notably in 2003, as part of efforts to enhance Mons’ heritage.

In 2015, it was one of the key venues for Mons European Capital of Culture. Today, the Royal Theatre is a major venue for artistic dissemination:

- Hosts local and international theatre productions.
- Organizes concerts, operas, and dance performances.
- Actively participates in the cultural season of Mars – Mons arts de la scène, which includes several cultural venues in the city.

The Royal Theatre of Mons remains a must-visit for art and culture lovers, offering a unique experience that blends history, architecture, and high-quality performances.
en.Tour Cesar:
The Caesar Tower of Mons is a medieval remnant located in the Château Park, near the belfry. It is one of the few surviving elements of the former Count's Castle of Mons, which served as the residence of the Counts of Hainaut from the 11th to the 15th century.

The Caesar Tower was built in the 13th or 14th century, probably during the reign of John of Avesnes, Count of Hainaut, who undertook a modernization of the castle enclosure at the end of the 13th century. It was located at the northeast end of the castle walls, complementing the Saint-Calixte chapel to the southwest. The entire enclosure measured about 280 meters in perimeter and surrounded an area of approximately 5,000 square meters.

Today, only the base of the Caesar Tower remains visible, integrated into the Château Park, a green space developed in the 19th century on the ruins of the Count's Castle.

In recent decades, efforts have been made to preserve and restore the remnants of the Caesar Tower. Archaeological investigations have been carried out to study its structure and evolution, notably in 2020, in preparation for restoration work aimed at stabilizing the tower and securing the site for visitors.

The Caesar Tower is an important witness to Mons' medieval past and its role as a center of power for the Counts of Hainaut. Though partially in ruins, it remains a significant element of the city's historical heritage.
en.Tour du Val des Ecoliers:
The Tour du Val des Écoliers (Tower of the Scholars' Valley) is a historic defensive tower located in Mons, Belgium. This medieval tower is part of the city's ancient fortifications and stands as a testament to Mons' strategic importance during the Middle Ages.

Built in the 14th century, the tower was part of the defensive system that protected the city of Mons. It is named after the nearby Val des Écoliers (Scholars' Valley), which was historically associated with educational institutions and religious communities.

The tower features typical medieval military architecture with thick stone walls, narrow windows designed for archers, and a solid construction meant to withstand attacks. Its strategic location allowed it to serve as both a defensive position and a lookout point for monitoring the surrounding area.

Today, the Tour du Val des Écoliers is a protected historical monument that contributes to Mons' rich architectural heritage. While it may not be open to the public for regular visits, it remains an important landmark that helps visitors understand the city's medieval past and its evolution from a fortified settlement to a modern urban center.

The tower serves as a reminder of Mons' historical role as a strategic military and administrative center in the region, and its preservation helps maintain the connection between the city's past and present.
en.Tour Valenciennoise:
The Tour Valenciennoise (Valenciennes Tower) is a historic defensive tower located in Mons, Belgium. This medieval tower is part of the city's ancient fortifications and stands as a testament to Mons' strategic importance during the Middle Ages.

Built in the 14th century, the tower was part of the defensive system that protected the city of Mons. It is named after the city of Valenciennes, which was historically an important administrative and military center in the region. The tower served as a key defensive position along the city's fortifications.

The tower features typical medieval military architecture with thick stone walls, narrow windows designed for archers, and a solid construction meant to withstand attacks. Its strategic location allowed it to serve as both a defensive position and a lookout point for monitoring the surrounding area.

Today, the Tour Valenciennoise is a protected historical monument that contributes to Mons' rich architectural heritage. While it may not be open to the public for regular visits, it remains an important landmark that helps visitors understand the city's medieval past and its evolution from a fortified settlement to a modern urban center.

The tower serves as a reminder of Mons' historical role as a strategic military and administrative center in the region, and its preservation helps maintain the connection between the city's past and present.
en.Tresors de Sainte Waudru:
The Treasures of Sainte Waudru are a remarkable collection of religious artifacts and precious objects housed in the Collegiate Church of Sainte-Waudru in Mons. This exceptional treasury represents centuries of devotion, artistic craftsmanship, and the rich religious heritage of the city.

The collection includes numerous precious objects dating from the Middle Ages to the present day, including reliquaries, liturgical vessels, vestments, and works of art. Among the most notable pieces are the reliquary of Sainte Waudru, containing the remains of the city's patron saint, and various objects used in religious ceremonies throughout the centuries.

The treasury also houses a collection of liturgical vestments, some of which are several centuries old and represent the finest examples of textile art from different periods. These vestments are still used during special religious ceremonies, particularly during the Ducasse de Mons celebrations.

Many of the objects in the treasury are made of precious materials such as gold, silver, and precious stones, reflecting the importance of the Collegiate Church as a center of religious and cultural life in Mons. The collection also includes works by renowned artists and craftsmen from different periods.

The Treasures of Sainte Waudru are not only of religious significance but also represent an important part of Mons' cultural heritage. They provide visitors with insight into the artistic and religious traditions that have shaped the city's identity over the centuries.

The treasury is carefully preserved and maintained, ensuring that these precious objects continue to be available for future generations to appreciate and study. Access to the treasury may be limited to guided tours or special occasions, allowing for proper conservation of these delicate and valuable artifacts.
en.Collegiale Sainte Waudru:
The Collegiate Church of Sainte-Waudru is one of the most magnificent religious buildings in Mons, Belgium, and a masterpiece of Gothic architecture. This imposing church, dedicated to the city's patron saint, Sainte Waudru, stands as a testament to the religious and cultural importance of Mons throughout the centuries.

Construction of the collegiate church began in the 15th century and continued over several centuries, resulting in a magnificent example of Brabant Gothic architecture. The church features soaring vaulted ceilings, intricate stone carvings, and magnificent stained glass windows that create an atmosphere of spiritual grandeur.

The church houses numerous artistic treasures, including the famous Car d'Or (Golden Chariot), a magnificent processional carriage used during the Ducasse de Mons celebrations. The treasury contains precious reliquaries, liturgical objects, and works of art that reflect the church's rich history and the devotion of generations of worshippers.

The Collegiate Church of Sainte-Waudru is particularly famous for its association with the Ducasse de Mons, the city's most important folk festival. During this annual celebration, the relics of Sainte Waudru are carried through the streets of Mons in the Car d'Or, following a tradition that dates back to the 14th century.

The church's architecture and artistic treasures make it a must-visit destination for anyone interested in religious art, medieval architecture, or the cultural heritage of Mons. The building continues to serve as an active place of worship while also welcoming visitors who come to admire its beauty and learn about its history.

The Collegiate Church of Sainte-Waudru is not just a religious building but a living symbol of Mons' identity and a testament to the city's enduring faith and cultural traditions.
en.Ancien Chateau Comtal:
The Ancien Château Comtal (Old Count's Castle) is a historic site in Mons that represents the medieval origins of the city. Although the castle itself no longer exists, the site where it once stood is of great historical and archaeological significance.

The castle was originally built in the 11th century and served as the residence of the Counts of Hainaut, who ruled over the region during the Middle Ages. It was strategically located on a hill overlooking the city, providing both protection and a symbol of the count's authority over the territory.

The castle complex included defensive walls, towers, living quarters, and administrative buildings. It was the center of political and military power in the region, and many important historical events took place within its walls. The castle also housed the chapel of Saint-Calixte, which still exists today and is one of the oldest religious buildings in Mons.

Over the centuries, the castle fell into disrepair and was eventually demolished. However, archaeological excavations have revealed important information about the castle's layout and the daily life of its inhabitants. The site continues to be of interest to historians and archaeologists studying the medieval history of Mons.

Today, the site of the Ancien Château Comtal is part of the Beffroi de Mons UNESCO World Heritage site. Visitors can see the remains of the castle's foundations and the chapel of Saint-Calixte, which provides a tangible connection to Mons' medieval past.

The Ancien Château Comtal represents an important chapter in Mons' history, serving as a reminder of the city's origins as a fortified settlement and its evolution into the modern city it is today.
en.Anciens Abattoirs:
The Anciens Abattoirs (Old Slaughterhouses) of Mons are historic industrial buildings that represent an important part of the city's economic and social history. These buildings, constructed in the 19th century, served as the main slaughterhouse facility for the city of Mons and the surrounding region.

The slaughterhouses were built to meet the growing demand for meat processing in the rapidly industrializing city. They were designed with practical considerations in mind, including proper drainage, ventilation, and facilities for the various stages of meat processing. The buildings reflect the industrial architecture of the period, with functional design taking precedence over aesthetic considerations.

The facility played an important role in the local economy, providing employment for many residents and ensuring a safe and regulated meat supply for the city. The slaughterhouses operated for many decades, serving the needs of the growing population of Mons and the surrounding area.

As the city modernized and food processing methods changed, the old slaughterhouses became obsolete and were eventually closed. However, the buildings remain as a testament to Mons' industrial heritage and the evolution of food processing technology.

Today, the Anciens Abattoirs are protected as part of Mons' industrial heritage. While they may not be open to the public for regular visits, they serve as an important reminder of the city's economic development and the role that industrial facilities played in shaping the urban landscape.

The preservation of these buildings helps maintain a connection to Mons' industrial past and provides valuable insight into how the city's economy and infrastructure have evolved over time.
en.Square:
Tucked away in the historic center of Mons, just a stone’s throw from the Grand-Place and the Belfry, Square Saint-Germain is a small, charming green space that’s full of character. 

Set on the former site of the Saint-Germain Collegiate Church—demolished during the French Revolution—this public garden tells part of the city’s long and layered story. 

The square was designed in 1887 by architects Joseph Hubert and Louis Fuchs, and has been renovated several times since, most recently between 2017 and 2021. Today, it offers a harmonious blend of old-world charm and modern comfort, with redesigned paths, updated urban furniture, inviting benches, and the shade of a majestic chestnut tree. 

A highlight of the square is its commemorative fountain dedicated to the Mons volunteers of the 1830 Belgian Revolution. Originally located on Place Régnier-au-Long-Col, it was moved here in 1930 to mark the centenary of the event. Quiet and dignified, the fountain honors the city’s enduring spirit of freedom and resilience. 

Far more than a shortcut or meeting point, Square Saint-Germain is now a true “urban oasis,” beloved by locals and visitors alike. 

Whether you're looking to take a peaceful break, enjoy a bit of greenery, or simply soak in the atmosphere, this hidden gem offers a welcome pause in the heart of the city.
en.Pilori:
Nestled at the foot of the majestic Sainte-Waudru Collegiate Church, on the quiet Place du Chapitre, stands an elegant and little-known fountain: the Pilori Fountain. 

Built in 1779 at the request of the church chapter, it replaced an older well that dated back to the 16th century. 

Designed by the architect Ouvertus, the fountain features a refined triangular shape in Louis XVI style, made from local blue limestone from Écaussinnes. 

Delicately sculpted pilasters frame the structure, which is topped by a slender pyramid, a decorative vase, and a banner bearing the chapter’s coat of arms—symbols of its ecclesiastical heritage. 

Unlike many purely decorative fountains, this one was fully functional, fed by an ancient aqueduct from the Lafond spring, and served as a valuable water source for the neighborhood. 

Classified as a historical monument in 1955, the fountain has been restored several times over the years to preserve its beauty and original details. 

Today, it remains one of the few 18th-century fountains still standing in Mons. 

Discreet and refined, the Pilori Fountain blends beautifully with the Gothic grandeur of Sainte-Waudru. It offers a striking contrast between medieval stone and 18th-century ornamentation—an evocative reminder of the city’s rich civic and religious heritage.

nl.Grand-place:
Deze tekst is afkomstig van de officiële website van de Stad Bergen en van visitMons.be.

In het hart van Bergen is de Grote Markt veel meer dan een openbaar plein: het is het levende symbool van de geschiedenis, cultuur en gezelligheid van de stad. Een echt architecturaal juweel dat getuigt van eeuwen stedelijke ontwikkeling en dat vandaag nog steeds het zenuwcentrum van de stad vormt. Omringd door historische gebouwen belichaamt het de ziel van Bergen en is het een onmisbare ontmoetingsplek voor zowel inwoners als bezoekers.

De Grote Markt wordt omzoomd door prachtige gevels in uiteenlopende stijlen, van gotiek tot neoclassicisme en renaissance. Een van de opvallendste gebouwen is het indrukwekkende stadhuis, gebouwd in de 15e eeuw in gotische stijl. De gevel, versierd met beeldhouwwerken, wordt bekroond door de beroemde 'Aap van de Grand'Garde', een symbool van geluk voor wie zijn hoofd met de linkerhand aait.

Tegenover het stadhuis staan andere opmerkelijke gebouwen, zoals het Huis van het Gulden Vlies en de voormalige Lakenhal, die bijdragen aan de elegantie van het plein. Elke steen vertelt een verhaal, elk architecturaal detail onthult een tijdperk.

De Grote Markt van Bergen is ook het toneel van talrijke evenementen die het stadsleven bepalen. Het bekendste is ongetwijfeld de Ducasse van Bergen, beter bekend als de Doudou, die op de lijst van immaterieel cultureel erfgoed van UNESCO staat. Dit jaarlijks festival trekt duizenden bezoekers die het legendarische gevecht tussen Sint-Joris en de Draak willen meemaken.

Het hele jaar door bruist het plein ook van markten, concerten, kerstactiviteiten en andere festiviteiten. De terrassen van cafés en restaurants nodigen uit tot ontspanning en bieden een prachtig uitzicht op de levendigheid van de plek.

Als historisch hart van Bergen is de Grote Markt het ideale vertrekpunt om de stad te verkennen. Ze ligt vlak bij tal van bezienswaardigheden, zoals het Belfort (UNESCO-werelderfgoed) en het Doudoumuseum.

De Grote Markt is het ware hart van de historische stad. Ze ligt dicht bij de winkelstraten en het belfort. Het plein is geplaveid zoals in oude steden en herbergt talrijke cafés en restaurants, evenals het stadhuis. De rand van het plein is toegankelijk voor auto's, maar stilstaan of rijden in het midden is verboden. Elk jaar vormt ze het toneel van het beroemde Lumeçongevecht tussen Sint-Joris en de Draak.

De Grote Markt beschikt ook over een fontein, ingehuldigd op 21 maart 2006. Tijdens de eindejaarsfeesten staat er ook de kerstmarkt "Mons, hart in de sneeuw". Hoewel de gebouwen ogenschijnlijk harmonieus zijn, weerspiegelen de gevels rond de Grote Markt de grote architecturale stromingen van de afgelopen eeuwen, waarin gotiek en moderniteit verrassend goed samengaan.

De gevel van het gebouw genaamd "Au Blan Levrie" illustreert hoe de Stad oud en nieuw wist te combineren. Het is een stenen gebouw volgens de voorschriften van de Stad om brand te voorkomen, vooral op de Grote Markt. Het werd gebouwd in 1530 in gotische stijl voor de rijke familie Malapert. In 1975 kregen de architecten A. Godart en O. Dupire de opdracht het gebouw in te richten als bank. Ze verwijderden volledig het interieur om het precies op te meten alvorens het restauratieproject uit te werken. De gevel kon volledig in haar oorspronkelijke staat worden hersteld.

Op de plek waar het Lumeçongevecht plaatsvindt, waarbij Sint-Joris op Drievuldigheidszondag de Draak een dodelijke slag toebrengt, is een ring van blauwe steen met een diameter van 12 meter in het plaveisel van de Grote Markt geïntegreerd, 's nachts verlicht.

Deze ring voegt zich bij de beelden van Gérard Garouste (de helling met de Draak en de helm van Sint-Joris) en wordt zo een permanent getuige van de rituele Ducasse van de stad Bergen.

Bovendien is deze stenen ring, een krachtig symbool van verbondenheid, gegraveerd met de namen van de 19 gemeenten die Bergen vormen. Van Hyon tot Jemappes, van Villers-Saint-Ghislain tot Maisières,... ze zijn er allemaal! Dit symbolische werk in het hart van de stad kostte 9.700 euro, ofwel 10% van het totale bedrag. Het overige werd gefinancierd door het EFRO van de Europese Unie en het Commissariaat-generaal voor Toerisme van het Waals Gewest in het kader van het toeristisch herwaarderingsbeleid van Bergen.

De Grote Markt van Bergen is meer dan een doorgangsplek: het is een plek waar geschiedenis, cultuur en gezelligheid elkaar ontmoeten. Kom deze unieke plek ontdekken, waar elk bezoek je onderdompelt in de ziel van de stad.


We kunnen nu doorgaan naar het volgende punt...
nl.Beffroi:
Het Belfort van Bergen, door de inwoners 'El Catiau' genoemd, is een emblematisch monument van de stad, bekend om zijn unieke architectuur en rijke geschiedenis.

Gebouwd tussen 1661 en 1672 naar de plannen van architect Louis Ledoux en voltooid door Vincent Anthony, is het Belfort van Bergen het enige barokke belfort in België. Het rijst 87 meter hoog op en torent uit boven de stad vanuit het kasteelpark, de vroegere locatie van het grafelijk kasteel.

Dit belfort verving de 'Torenuurwerk', die in 1661 werd vernietigd. Het diende oorspronkelijk als uitkijktoren, openbare klok en waarschuwingssysteem bij gevaar, zoals brand of invasie.

Het Belfort bevat een beiaard met 49 klokken, die het leven in Bergen ritmeren met traditionele melodieën zoals 'El Grosse Cloche du Catiau', 'La Bière', 'Zandrine' en 'Le Canson du Ropieur'. Elk kwartier klinken deze melodieën door de stad, als gekoesterde traditie.

Door de eeuwen heen werd het belfort aangetast door tijd en weersinvloeden. In de jaren 1980 werd een grootschalige restauratie gestart en voltooid in 2015. Deze stabiliseerde de structuur, herstelde architecturale elementen en creëerde een museumruimte.

In 1999 werd het Belfort van Bergen opgenomen op de Werelderfgoedlijst van UNESCO, samen met andere belforten in België en Frankrijk, vanwege zijn historische en architecturale waarde.

Victor Hugo omschreef het Belfort bij zijn bezoek aan Bergen in 1837 als 'een enorme koffiepot, geflankeerd door vier kleinere theepotten', waarmee hij zijn bijzondere architectuur illustreerde.

In tegenstelling tot veel Vlaamse belforten heeft het geen gevangenis of gerechtelijke zalen, wat het uniek maakt.

Het Belfort is open voor het publiek en biedt een interpretatiecentrum gewijd aan zijn geschiedenis en erkenning door UNESCO. Met een panoramische lift kunnen bezoekers naar de top voor een indrukwekkend uitzicht over Bergen en omgeving.

Het Belfort van Bergen is meer dan een toren: het is een levend symbool van de geschiedenis, cultuur en identiteit van de stad. Zijn majestueuze silhouet waakt nog steeds over Bergen en herinnert voortdurend aan het rijke verleden.
nl.Hotel de Ville: Het Stadhuis van Bergen, gelegen op de Grote Markt, is een architecturaal en historisch symbool van de stad. Hoofdzakelijk gebouwd in de 15e eeuw, getuigt het van de Brabantse gotische stijl en van de stedelijke evolutie van Bergen.
nl.Singe du Grand Garde:
Deze tekst is geschreven door de heer Benoît VAN CAENEGEM, conservator van de Collegiale Sint-Waltrudiskerk en haar schat.

De Aap van de Grand'Garde – geluksbrenger van de stad Bergen.

Vastgemaakt aan de gevel van het Stadhuis van Bergen, is het waarschijnlijk de oudste en beroemdste inwoner van de stad. Zijn glimmende hoofd wordt voortdurend geaaid door zowel inwoners als toeristen. Elke belangrijke bezoeker van de stad wordt uitgenodigd hem te begroeten bij aankomst.

Men zegt dat hij – zoals sommige lokale auteurs beweren – 'de Lumeçon al meer dan honderd keer heeft gezien'.

Hij? De Aap van de Grand’Garde, natuurlijk!

Het beeld stelt een hurkende, guitige aap voor die met zijn linkerhand op zijn wang krabt – bekend ver buiten zijn verblijfplaats. Maar wat weten we nu echt over dit beeldje dat al zo veel inkt heeft doen vloeien?

Zijn oorsprong blijft onzeker. Er bestaan drie gangbare hypotheses in Bergen:

• Hij is misschien het meesterstuk van een smid die toegang wilde krijgen tot het gilde. De aap was in de middeleeuwen immers een populair dier.

• Hij is misschien het uithangbord van een herberg die zich in de kelders van het stadhuis bevond. Deze 'herberg' verdween in 1897 of rond 1903-1904.

• Hij is misschien ook een soort schandpaal voor 'drukke' kinderen. In het Borinage zei men vroeger: Als je niet braaf bent, breng ik je naar de aap van Bergen.

Welke hypothese is het meest geloofwaardig? Geen? Alle drie? Dat laat men aan ieder over. Niemand kan met zekerheid zeggen wat de oorsprong is van dit beeldje, zo dierbaar aan het hart van de inwoners en bezoekers.

Toch is er één zekerheid: sinds 1930 wordt hij beschouwd als geluksbrenger van de stad Bergen – op voorwaarde dat je zijn hoofd met je linkerhand aait. Deze traditie werd bedacht door Paul Heupgen (1868–1949).

Hij schreef erover in een van zijn 'Viéseries':

‘In werkelijkheid weet men niet waar hij vandaan komt, noch waarom hij daar is. Er zijn hypotheses, maar geen enkele zekerheid. Behalve deze: wie hem aait, is verzekerd van geluk, minstens voor een jaar.’
nl.Statue du Dragon:
De twee bronzen beelden van de geharnaste Sint-Joris en de draak, rechts van de hoofdingang van het Stadhuis van Bergen, zijn het werk van de Franse beeldhouwer Gérard Garouste. Deze werken werden ingehuldigd in 2006 en bevinden zich aan de voet van de trappen die leiden naar een van de ingangen van het stadhuis.

Gérard Garouste maakte ook een muurschildering voor de trouwzaal van het Stadhuis.

Deze eigentijdse beelden sluiten aan bij de traditie van het gevecht tussen Sint-Joris en de draak, een legendarisch verhaal dat elk jaar wordt herdacht tijdens de Ducasse van Bergen.
nl.Statue Saint Georges:
Het standbeeld van Sint-Joris nabij het station van Bergen is een hedendaags kunstwerk dat in 2024 werd ingehuldigd op het Congresplein, gelegen achter het nieuwe station dat werd ontworpen door de architect Santiago Calatrava. Dit bronzen beeldhouwwerk, gemaakt door de Franse kunstenaar Xavier Veilhan, stelt de emblematische strijd voor van Sint-Joris tegen de draak, een mythische basis van de identiteit van de stad Bergen.

Het beeld maakt deel uit van het project "Kunst woont in de Stad", dat tot doel heeft kunst te integreren in de openbare ruimte van Bergen. Met een kostprijs van 450.000 euro, deels gefinancierd met regionale en Europese subsidies, riep het werk gemengde reacties op. Sommige inwoners spraken hun teleurstelling uit over het uiterlijk van het beeld, dat werd vergeleken met onverwachte populaire figuren, terwijl anderen de uitgaven voor het kunstproject bekritiseerden.

Xavier Veilhan koos voor een gestileerde en vereenvoudigde benadering om Sint-Joris en de draak voor te stellen. De kunstenaar verklaarde dat hij een "vreemd en verontrustend" werk wilde maken, door de vormen te verzachten en details weg te laten om een meer dromerige visie van de legende te bieden.

Dit standbeeld van Sint-Joris verrijkt het stedelijk landschap van Bergen met een hedendaagse interpretatie van een legendarische figuur die de inwoners nauw aan het hart ligt. Het getuigt van de wil van de stad om kunst in de openbare ruimte te integreren, terwijl het tegelijk het debat stimuleert over de plaats van kunst en de financiering ervan in de samenleving.
nl.Fontaine du Rouge Puits:
De Rode Put in Bergen, ook wel de Put van de Kruidermarkt genoemd, dateert uit 1833 en is het werk van de Bergense architect Albert J.B. Jamot (1808-1874), laureaat van de Prijs van Rome voor architectuur in 1828.

Zijn bijnaam ‘Rode Put’ komt voort uit het feit dat hij gedurende een bepaalde periode volledig rood werd geschilderd. Boven op de massieve gebogen zuil, op het trapvormige hoofdgestel, bevindt zich een stenen vaas. Op elke zijde laat een mascaron – waarop een grote gebogen buis is bevestigd – water stromen in vier stenen bakken. Aan de voet van de basis, omringd door twee trappen, worden vier stenen bollen toegevoegd aan het geheel.

Zijn naam verwijst dus naar de tijd dat hij rood geschilderd was. Verplaatst in 1877 naar de Kruidermarkt, werd hij in 1981 terug op zijn oorspronkelijke locatie geplaatst.
nl.Immeuble Blanc Lévrier:
Het gebouw genaamd "Au Blanc Lévrier", gelegen op nummer 35 van de Grote Markt in Bergen, is een van de architecturale parels van de stad. Opgetrokken in 1530 voor Jean le Bouvier en de familie Malapert, weerspiegelt dit herenhuis de burgerlijke gotiek van de 16e eeuw, een tijd waarin natuursteen voornamelijk werd gereserveerd voor religieuze of militaire gebouwen.

De gevel in blauwe hardsteen valt op door zijn vier traveeën en drie verdiepingen, versierd met spitsbogen en fijne lijstwerken. Twee beelden van windhonden – symbolen van loyaliteit en trouw – omlijsten het opschrift "AV BLAN LEVRIE", wat het gebouw zijn naam geeft. Heraldische schilden, waaronder dat van Karel V met de tweekoppige adelaar en de ketting van het Gulden Vlies, sieren ook de gevel en getuigen van de keizerlijke invloed in die tijd.

Geklasseerd als historisch monument in 1952 en erkend als uitzonderlijk erfgoed van Wallonië in 2016, heeft het gebouw verschillende transformaties ondergaan. In 1975 werd het gerestaureerd door de architecten Godart en Dupire om er een bank in onder te brengen, waarbij de gevel werd behouden en discrete eigentijdse elementen werden geïntegreerd, zoals stalen raamkozijnen met slanke profielen.
